VHM's Fiscal H1 Loss Widens; Raises 'Going Concern' Doubts
data.description:
VHM reported that its loss per diluted share in the fiscal first half widened to AU$0.03 from AU$0.02 a year earlier, according to a March 7 filing with the Australian bourse.

Income for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$93,000, down from AU$470,000 in the same period a year earlier.

The critical minerals company said it cannot assure securing further financing in the future and raised concerns about its ability to continue as a "going concern."

data.title: Boss Would Fund A$8M of Exploration Over Seven Years Over Two Stages
Australia Shares Set to Pause Recent Decline — Market Talk
data.description: Australia's S&P/ASX 200 looks set to bounce after finishing last week at its lowest closing level since August. ASX futures are up by 0.9%, suggesting the benchmark index should recover some of Friday's 1.8% fall. The ASX 200's worst day since September wrapped up a third consecutive weekly fall. With little economic data due this week and Australia's central bank not set to announce its next interest rate decision until next month, investors may remain focused on global geopolitical and trade worries. Major U.S. indices pared their recent losses on Friday, although they still finished lower for the week. The DJIA added 0.5%, the S&P 500 gained 0.6%, and the Nasdaq Composite put on 0.7%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Boss Has Further Option to Purchase an Additional 10% Interest in Project
Vacancy Rate Across Auckland's Central Business District Rises to Nearly 14% in H2 2024
data.description:
The vacancy rate across Auckland's central business district rose to almost 14% in the six months to December 2024 from a little over 13% in the prior half-year period, according to a study by Colliers International published March 6.

Prime-grade vacancy, which is a combination of A-grade and premium space, grew to 9.8% from 7.5%. Tenants such as BNZ and Beca took advantage of new developments, which allowed them to "move to higher grade space, relocate to a favored precinct, and to optimize their office footprint," per the report.

Tenants continued to prefer top-quality space amid business efforts to entice employees back into the office. Features such as office floors with an existing fit-out were particularly popular among prospective tenants, per the report.

"Being able to lease space such as this holds appeal for firms because they can quickly establish their operations and avoid extensive costs when relocating," said Rob Bird, national director of office leasing at Colliers.

data.title: Dreadnought Resources' Fiscal H1 Loss Widens
data.description:
Dreadnought Resources reported that loss per diluted share in the fiscal first half widened to AU$0.0038 from AU$0.001 a year earlier, according to a March 7 filing with the Australian bourse.

Interest income for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$2,455, down from AU$115,727 in the same period a year earlier.

data.title: Income Asset Management Enters Mediation With BGC Partners Over Legal Dispute
data.description:
Income Asset Management Group moved into confidential mediation as part of its ongoing legal proceedings with BGC Partners (BGC), trading as Fixed Income Solutions, according to a March 7 filing with the Australian bourse.

The lawsuit alleges that two of IAM's employees breached restraint of trade provisions in their previous employment contracts with BGC, the filing said.

IAM is accused of inducing these breaches, and BGC is seeking damages and costs, the filing added.

The company denies the claims and is vigorously defending the case, continuing to incur legal expenses while its insurer reviews the policy coverage, per the filing.

In the same filing, IAM said it transitioned nearly AU$1.8 billion of client bond and loan assets to Perpetual Corporate Trust, expected to boost its annual financial performance by AU$600,000 annually from fiscal 2026.

data.title: Fed's Daly said growing uncertainty among businesses could slow demand in the US economy
data.description:
Mary Daly, President of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco, stated that rising uncertainty among businesses could dampen demand in the U.S. economy but does not justify a change in interest rates.

  • business leaders in her district report heightened uncertainty about the economy and policy, which research suggests can reduce demand
  • Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) does not need to adjust rates when it meets next week
  • believes current interest rates are appropriate
  • believes the economy remains strong

Instead of reacting to short-term news or market fluctuations, she advocates for a more measured approach to evaluating economic conditions.Her remarks align with comments from Fed Chair Jerome Powell, who also stated on Friday that policymakers do not need to rush any changes despite growing uncertainty.

  • Fed's Powell: U.S. central bank does not need to be in a hurry to adjust interest rates
  • Q&A from Fed's Powell: No need to redefine price stability

--

Mary Daly

Daly's remarks from her LinkedIn post on Friday.

Weekend - Lutnick says Trump sticking to US aluminum-steel tariff timeline
data.description:
U.S. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick indicated that the 25% tariffs on steel and aluminum imports, set to take effect on Wednesday, are unlikely to be postponed.

  • When asked on NBC’s Meet the Press over the weekend if the tariffs would go into effect on March 12 as planned, Lutnick confirmed, “Yes.”

The tariffs, imposed by President Donald Trump in February, cover imports from key suppliers like Canada and Mexico and extend to finished metal products. While U.S. steelmakers have urged Trump to maintain the levies without exemptions, the measures could impact American businesses that rely on these materials.

Officials say the policy aims to curb efforts by countries such as Russia and China to circumvent existing trade restrictions.

---

Trump’s approach to tariffs—often characterized by abrupt announcements, reversals, and delays—has created uncertainty and market volatility.

Oil producers starting to hold back capital - "the opposite of what the president wants"
data.description:
A few items via Reuters over the weekend ahead of Ceraweek, where energy industry leaders meet in Houston.

Josh Young, chief investment officer at Bison Interests.:

  • "The costs are way higher and that affects the profitability,
  • "You're starting to see producers hold back their capital. It's the opposite of what the president wants."

Dan Pickering, chief investment officer at Pickering Energy Partners.

  • "Shareholders are saying capital discipline, return cash to shareholders and then you have the most powerful man in the world, saying, drill, baby, drill - I think you pay lip service to the president, and you follow the wishes of the shareholder"

What is it they say? The best cure for low oil prices is low oil prices.

  • ie - when the cycle sees job cuts, capex pulled back etc then that'll lead to tighter supply and higher prices. The key idea to keep in mind is 'eventually', K?

---

CERAWeek is an annual energy conference organized by S&P Global

  • brings together global leaders, policymakers, executives, and experts from the energy, technology, and financial sectors
  • CERAWeek 2025 is scheduled for March 10-14

data.title: Myer Holdings to Join S&P/ASX 300 Index
data.description: Myer Holdings will join the S&P/ASX 300 Index prior to market open on March 24, according to a March 7 filing with the Australian bourse.

data.title: Australia shares set to rise at open; NZ slightly up
data.description:
Australian shares were poised to rise on Monday, with probable gains in commodity stocks, while investors continue to navigate global market uncertainty amid the ongoing trade war between major economies.

The local share price index futures (YAPcm1) rose 0.9%, a 62.8-point premium to the underlying S&P/ASX 200 index close. The benchmark fell 1.8% to close below the psychologically crucial 8,000 mark on Friday.

New Zealand's benchmark S&P/NZX 50 index rose 0.1% in early trading.

data.title: Astral Resources Extends Takeover Offer for Maximus Resources
data.description: Astral Resources NL extended its off-market takeover offer for Maximus Resources to March 21 from the original deadline of March 7, according to a filing with the Australian Securities Exchange on March 7.
